title: Psychology-Driven Enhancement of Humour Translation
research_questions: Can a Chain-of-Thought Humour Decomposition Mechanism improve humour translation quality by reducing humour loss and linguistic interference?
Does HDM work well on other datasets?
Does HDM work well on other language?
Does HDM work well on open-source models?
How do Humour Theory and the Humour Decomposition Mechanism affect the results in ablation?
Is zero-shot HDM robust to different humour translation prompts?

datasets_used.name: Short Jokes Dataset
datasets_used.size: 500 samples randomly selected for experiments; 100 samples randomly selected for ablation/generality analyses

methods_proposed.name: Humour Decomposition Mechanism (HDM)
methods_proposed.summary: HDM is a three-step Chain-of-Thought prompting paradigm for humour translation: first mine intrinsic knowledge or analysis of the source joke, then translate that analysis into the target language, and finally compose a new target-language joke from the translated analysis.

methods_proposed.name: Humour-theory-enhanced HDM
methods_proposed.summary: The paper integrates a humour theory prompt inspired by Toplyn’s joke-generation structure, requiring the model to decompose each joke into topic, angle, and punchline before translation and recomposition.

humor_theories.detail: Toplyn’s monologue joke generation theory is used to define joke structure as topic, angle, and punchline; these fields are explicitly requested in the Humour Decomposition prompt.
The related work states that incongruity theory views humour as arising from incongruity between readers’ expectations and the ending of a story.

evaluation_methods.detail: GEMBA is used as a GPT4-based no-reference evaluator for humour, fluency, and coherence, with prompts modified for humour translation evaluation.

GEMBA-SQM scores scalar quality from 0 to 100, and GEMBA-STARS scores one-to-five star rankings; reported as SQM-H, SQM-F, SQM-C and STAR-H, STAR-F, STAR-C. Results are averaged over three runs, with invalid responses omitted.

headline_results.finding: The paper reports average gains for HDM of 7.75% in humour, 2.81% in fluency, and 6.13% in coherence for generated translated text.
headline_results.value: 7.75% in humour; 2.81% in fluency; 6.13% in coherence

headline_results.finding: On English-to-Chinese Short Jokes, GPT4-Turbo + HDM achieved the best Table 1 scores across all reported humour, fluency, and coherence metrics.
headline_results.value: SQM-H 70.54; STAR-H 3.45; SQM-F 99.45; STAR-F 4.99; SQM-C 97.73; STAR-C 4.96

headline_results.finding: For GPT4-Turbo on English-to-Chinese, HDM beat MAPS by +11.20 SQM-H, +4.33 SQM-F, and +9.11 SQM-C.
On other datasets, HDM improved over the MAPS baseline across all LLMs and metrics, with improvements of at least 1.84% in humour, 1.7% in fluency, and 2.15% in coherence.
headline_results.value: at least 1.84% in humour; 1.7% in fluency; 2.15% in coherence

headline_results.finding: On English-to-German with Yi-Large, the paper reports HDM improvements of 2.75% in humour, 3.25% in fluency, and 3% in coherence.
headline_results.value: 2.75% in humour; 3.25% in fluency; 3% in coherence

headline_results.finding: In open-source model tests, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct improved under HDM, while Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct lost humour score.
headline_results.value: Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct: SQM-H 51.60 to 61.80, SQM-F 91.40 to 97.40, SQM-C 86.00 to 91.35; Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct SQM-H 39.80 to 28.70

key_findings: HDM outperforms direct LLM translation, DUAL-REFLECT, and MAPS on Table 1 for all four closed-source backbone models across humour, fluency, and coherence metrics.
The strongest closed-source result is GPT4-Turbo + HDM, which reaches SQM-H 70.54, SQM-F 99.45, and SQM-C 97.73 on English-to-Chinese Short Jokes.
HDM generalizes beyond the main Short Jokes setup to Question-Answer Jokes and SemEval-2021 samples, where it improves every reported model/metric pair over the MAPS baseline.

The method also improves English-to-Spanish and English-to-German translation in nearly all reported settings, although Yi-Large EN→SP fluency is slightly lower with HDM than the baseline: 96.30 vs 96.50.
Ablation shows that HDM is the main contributor, and the topic-angle-punchline humour theory component works better when combined with HDM than when used without HDM.
Open-source models show mixed behavior: larger Qwen2.5 models improve, but smaller models can decline, especially on SQM-H.
limitations: The study is limited to four languages: Chinese, English, Spanish, and German.
All datasets selected in the study are in English, and future work is needed for additional translation directions.
The evaluation lacks human evaluation, even though the paper states human evaluation remains the gold standard for natural language generation quality.
The paper relies on GPT4-based GEMBA automatic metrics, which may introduce bias into the final results.
The applicability of HDM to different types of humour has not been systematically evaluated.
HDM struggles with puns that depend on pronunciation, character shape, and cultural or linguistic elements, such as the “Fleece Navidad” example.
For smaller open-source models, the paper observes a significant decline in evaluation scores, especially SQM-H.
